medications.indication_rationale: CEASE hydralazine — it is the offending agent driving the drug-induced lupus reaction, and stopping it is the single definitive intervention; the syndrome resolves over days to weeks after cessation. The AI Doctor should advise stopping it now and ensure urgent GP review arranges a safe antihypertensive alternative so blood pressure is not left unmanaged.
medications.deprescribing_note: Stop hydralazine immediately as the suspected cause of the reaction; do NOT simply substitute another drug remotely — the GP selects and titrates a replacement antihypertensive at the urgent in-person review, and documents the adverse drug reaction.
contraindications_in_this_case: Continued use is contraindicated once drug-induced lupus is suspected

medications.indication_rationale: Symptomatic relief of myalgia, arthralgia and fever while the drug reaction settles. Take with food and a full glass of water to protect the stomach. The source note prescribed an oral NSAID but did not specify a dose — a specific dose has NOT been synthesised here (see transform_flags); the prescriber sets the dose considering renal function and BP.
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution/avoid if renal impairment, active peptic ulcer disease, heart failure, or poorly controlled hypertension is present — to be checked at the in-person review before ongoing use
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: NSAID + antihypertensive therapy: NSAIDs can blunt antihypertensive effect and impair renal function — relevant in a hypertensive patient whose BP medication is being changed; use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time.

medications.indication_rationale: There is no septic focus and the patient is haemodynamically stable; empirical IV antibiotics and hospital admission for presumed sepsis would be over-treatment of a stable drug reaction. Antibiotics are only warranted if the same-day assessment identifies infection.

Mild transient hydralazine DILE resolves on drug cessation; systemic steroids are reserved for severe manifestations (e.g. significant serositis) and are not first-line for mild constitutional/myalgic disease. Not something the AI Doctor should initiate remotely.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop taking hydralazine now and do not take any further doses; bring the packet to the urgent review so the reaction can be documented.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the offending drug is what resolves the reaction and prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the anti-inflammatory strictly with food and a full glass of water; rest today and avoid overexertion.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Protects the stomach lining and manages myalgia/fever while the reaction settles.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Arrange for someone to check in given she lives alone and is unsteady; keep a phone within reach and clear walkways.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Mitigates the falls risk from acute weakness in a person living alone.

patient_education_points.point: Your symptoms look like a reaction of your immune system to a blood-pressure medicine (hydralazine). Stopping the medicine stops the reaction, and it usually settles over days to a few weeks.
Do not take any more hydralazine. You need to be seen in person urgently today so you can be examined and have blood tests to make sure this isn't an infection and to confirm the diagnosis.
You still need your blood pressure treated — your GP will start a safe alternative medicine at the review. Don't just stop treating your blood pressure without that follow-up.
Use the anti-inflammatory for the muscle pain with food and water. Rest today.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment; then GP review within 24–48 hours for the antihypertensive switch and to review ANA / anti-histone results
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Vital signs and haemodynamic stability, exclusion of infection, blood pressure control on a replacement agent, resolution of myalgia/fever, and serology results
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any chest pain, breathlessness, spreading or blistering rash, fever above 39 °C, worsening weakness, dizziness on standing, or a fall — seek urgent care immediately

must_include_items: Ask about recently-started medications and identify hydralazine begun ~8 weeks ago as the likely driver
Advise ceasing hydralazine now as the definitive step
Keep serious infection on the differential and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (examination + observations + bloods) rather than remote reassurance
Ensure blood pressure is not left unmanaged — GP to start a safe alternative antihypertensive
Give clear red-flag safety-netting (chest pain, breathlessness, spreading rash, fever >39 °C, worsening weakness/falls)
acceptable_alternatives: Symptomatic paracetamol instead of/alongside an NSAID for fever and myalgia is reasonable, particularly if NSAID caution applies
Referral to urgent care or a bulk-billing same-day clinic in place of the usual GP for the in-person assessment
errors_of_omission: Never asking about new medications and so missing the drug link
Failing to arrange in-person assessment for a systemically unwell patient telehealth cannot examine
Leaving blood pressure untreated with no follow-up plan
errors_of_commission: Escalating to a 000 ambulance / ED sepsis pathway with IV antibiotics for a haemodynamically stable patient with no septic focus
Prescribing or blind-switching a new antihypertensive remotely without examination
Initiating systemic corticosteroids for mild transient DILE
Inventing a specific NSAID dose not provided by the source
Reassuring the patient it is 'just a virus' and advising simple home rest with no in-person review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) advising cessation of hydralazine, (b) arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment while keeping infection on the differential, and (c) not over-escalating to an ambulance/ED sepsis work-up. Missing the medication link OR either mis-triage direction is a failing pattern.
